title: Project Selection & Business-Case Math
summary: How to choose which projects to fund and the numbers behind the business case, broken into scan-friendly blocks: the two method families, a formula bank for NPV, IRR, BCR, ROI, payback and EMV, a decision-rule table for picking a project, present-vs-future value, make-or-buy, scoring models, and the exam cues. Tailored to PMBOK 8, where sound project selection supports the Value principle.

sections.heading: Selection supports the Value principle
sections.body:
Funding is limited, so the organisation must back the projects with the best value and strongest strategic fit. In PMBOK 8 this is the Value principle in action: choose and justify work by the value it delivers. It is also a Business-Environment concern, and the 2026 ECO raises Business Environment to 26 percent of the exam.

For the exam you mostly compare candidate projects using benefit-measurement figures and apply a clear decision rule to each.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Ranks candidate projects using numbers.
Compare and pick the best figure.

groups.label: Includes
lines: NPV, IRR, BCR, ROI.
Payback period.
EMV and weighted scoring models.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Mathematical programming to allocate scarce resources across many options.

groups.label: Includes
lines: Linear programming.
Integer programming.
Rarely calculated on the exam - just recognise the name.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Benefit-measurement = comparative (rank the numbers).
Constrained-optimisation = mathematical programming.
Most exam questions are benefit-measurement: read the figure, apply the decision rule.

sections.heading: Business-case formulae
sections.body: Each formula sits next to its meaning and exam cue. You rarely compute these by hand on the exam, but you must know the direction each one points.

lines: Present value of a future amount.
r = discount rate per period, n = number of periods.
Discounting (dividing) pulls future cash back to today.
Future value of a present amount.
Compounding (multiplying) is the reverse of discounting.

items.formula: NPV = sum of discounted inflows - discounted outflows
lines: Net present value in money terms.
Higher NPV is better; NPV greater than 0 is viable.
Already accounts for the cost of capital - most trusted measure.
Return expressed as a percentage.
Higher IRR is better; clears the bar when it exceeds the required rate of return.

items.formula: BCR = PV of benefits / PV of costs
lines: Benefit-cost ratio.
Greater than 1 = benefits exceed costs (good).
A COST ratio (costs over benefits) inverts this - below 1 is good.
Return on investment as a percentage.
Higher ROI is better.

items.formula: Payback = time to recover the original investment
lines: Shorter payback is better.
Ignores time value of money and cash after recovery.
Expected monetary value of an uncertain outcome.
Sum across outcomes for a single expected figure.

blocks.title: Formula tip
lines: Money answer -> NPV. Percentage answer -> IRR or ROI.
Ratio answer -> BCR. Time answer -> payback.
Uncertainty weighted by probability -> EMV.

sections.heading: Which project to pick
sections.body: When a question lists several projects with figures, apply the direction rule and choose the favourable one.

rows.value: Higher is better. Pick the highest NPV. Greater than 0 = viable; negative = reject.
Higher is better. Pick the highest IRR. Must exceed the required rate of return.
Higher is better. Greater than 1 = viable; equal to 1 = break-even; below 1 = reject.
Higher is better. Pick the highest ROI.
Shorter is better. Pick the fastest payback.
For opportunities, higher (more positive) is better; for threats, lower expected cost is better.
If the ratio is costs over benefits, the rule flips: LOWER is better.

blocks.title: Exam traps
lines: Ignore the raw project duration - a longer project can still win on NPV.
IRR and NPV can occasionally rank projects differently; NPV is the tie-breaker.
A COST-benefit ratio inverts the 'greater than 1' logic - read the wording.
Payback is a liquidity screen only, never the deciding figure over NPV or IRR.

sections.heading: How each figure behaves
sections.body: The detail behind each decision rule, and why NPV is the most trusted comparative measure.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Discounts all future inflows and outflows to today and sums them.
Captures the time value of money.

groups.label: Rules
lines: Pick the HIGHER NPV.
Greater than 0 = viable.
Negative = destroys value, reject.

groups.label: What it is
lines: The discount rate at which NPV equals exactly 0.
Return as a percentage, not a money amount.

groups.label: Rules
lines: Higher IRR is better.
Clears the bar when it exceeds the required rate of return.
A short efficient project can beat a long one with a bigger absolute return.

groups.label: What it is
lines: PV of benefits divided by PV of costs.

groups.label: Rules
lines: Greater than 1 = benefits exceed costs, viable.
Equal to 1 = break-even.
Less than 1 = reject.
A cost-over-benefit ratio flips the logic.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Time to recover the original investment from net cash inflows.
Shorter = lower exposure, cash back sooner.

groups.label: Weaknesses
lines: Ignores the time value of money.
Ignores cash flows after recovery.
Use as a quick liquidity screen, not the deciding figure.

blocks.title: Why NPV leads
blocks.text: NPV already accounts for the cost of capital, so it is the most trusted comparative measure. When methods disagree, favour NPV.

sections.heading: Make-or-buy analysis
sections.body: Make-or-buy analysis compares producing or delivering something in-house against purchasing it externally. It feeds the procurement decision in the business case.

blocks.title: Golden rule
blocks.text: EXCLUDE sunk costs. Money already spent is gone and must not influence whether to make or buy going forward - only future costs and benefits count.

rows.value: Direct and indirect cost over the full life cycle.
Intellectual property and who owns the result.
Risk, control, and long-term strategic fit.

sections.heading: EMV and scoring models
sections.body: When choices carry uncertainty or value is not purely financial, use these to rank options.

items.title: List the criteria
lines: Strategic fit, cost, risk, benefit and so on.

items.title: Score each project per criterion
lines: Rate every candidate against each criterion.

items.title: Apply a weight
lines: Weight each criterion by importance.

items.title: Sum and rank
lines: Highest weighted total wins.
Useful when value is not purely financial.

blocks.title: EMV reminder
lines: Expected Monetary Value weights each outcome's value by its probability to give a single expected figure.
EMV = Probability x Impact; sum across outcomes.
See the PMP Formulas guide for the full worked calculation.

sections.heading: Exam hooks
sections.body: Across every method the exam hooks are the same: know the decision direction and never let sunk costs sway a forward-looking decision.

blocks.title: Memorise the directions
lines: Higher NPV wins; NPV greater than 0 is viable.
Higher IRR is better; it is the rate where NPV equals 0.
BCR greater than 1 is good; a cost-benefit ratio flips it.
Higher ROI is better.
Shorter payback is better, but it ignores time value and later cash flows.
Ignore sunk costs; decide make-or-buy on future cost, capability, IP and risk.
PV = FV / (1 + r)^n - discount before you compare.
Sound selection serves the PMBOK 8 Value principle.

source: PM Cram study notes - restructured into blocks and aligned to PMBOK 8 principles for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
